flashcards.front: What is the formal definition of Big O notation O(f(n))?
flashcards.back: A function g(n) is in O(f(n)) if there exist positive constants c and n₀ such that g(n) ≤ c × f(n) for all n ≥ n₀. It provides an upper bound on growth rate.

flashcards.front: What is Big Omega notation Ω(f(n)) used for?
flashcards.back: Big Omega provides a lower bound on function growth. g(n) ∈ Ω(f(n)) if there exist c > 0 and n₀ such that g(n) ≥ c × f(n) for all n ≥ n₀.
Big Theta provides a tight bound. g(n) ∈ Θ(f(n)) if g(n) is both O(f(n)) and Ω(f(n)), meaning it grows at the same rate as f(n) asymptotically.

flashcards.front: What does Little O notation o(f(n)) represent?
flashcards.back: Little o represents a strict upper bound. g(n) ∈ o(f(n)) means g(n) grows strictly slower than f(n); the ratio g(n)/f(n) → 0 as n → ∞.
Little omega represents a strict lower bound. g(n) ∈ ω(f(n)) means g(n) grows strictly faster than f(n); the ratio g(n)/f(n) → ∞ as n → ∞.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between time complexity and space complexity?
flashcards.back: Time complexity measures the number of basic operations an algorithm performs relative to input size. Space complexity measures the amount of memory an algorithm uses relative to input size.
Worst-case complexity represents the maximum time/space required for any input of size n. It provides a guarantee on performance and is most commonly used for analysis.
Best-case complexity represents the minimum time/space required for any input of size n. It describes how the algorithm performs with the most favorable input.
Average-case complexity represents the expected time/space over all possible inputs of size n, often assuming a uniform distribution of inputs.

flashcards.front: List the common complexity classes in increasing order.
flashcards.back: Constant O(1) < Logarithmic O(log n) < Linear O(n) < Linearithmic O(n log n) < Quadratic O(n²) < Cubic O(n³) < Exponential O(2ⁿ) < Factorial O(n!)

flashcards.front: Why are asymptotic notations important in algorithm analysis?
flashcards.back: They provide a standardized, machine-independent way to compare algorithm efficiency, focusing on growth rate rather than implementation details or hardware specifics.

flashcards.front: What does O(n²) mean in simple terms?
flashcards.back: It means the algorithm's running time grows proportionally to the square of input size. If input doubles, time roughly quadruples.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between asymptotic upper bound and tight bound?
flashcards.back: Asymptotic upper bound (Big O) may not be tight. Tight bound (Theta) exactly characterizes the growth rate. We say f(n) = O(g(n)) is tight if Ω(g(n)) also holds.
Space complexity measures the total amount of memory needed by an algorithm to execute, including input space and auxiliary space (extra memory used).
